definition: Sorting algorithms arrange the elements of a collection into a defined order (usually ascending by a key). Comparison-based sorts have a Ω(n log n) lower bound; specialized algorithms can do better when keys have structure (counting/radix sort).
layman: Sorting is putting cards in order. Different methods are like different sorting strategies — slot each card into a hand (insertion), split and merge halves (merge), or pivot around one card and recurse (quick).
content:
## Sorting algorithms

### Comparison vs non-comparison sorts

- **Comparison sorts** (`<` between any two items) — bounded below by Ω(n log n) in the worst case. Includes mergesort, heapsort, quicksort, Timsort.
- **Non-comparison sorts** (counting, radix, bucket) — can be O(n + k) when the key alphabet is small or fits in a small range.

In Python, `sorted(...)` and `list.sort()` use **Timsort** — an adaptive hybrid of mergesort and insertion-sort that exploits already-sorted runs in the input.

### The classics at a glance

| Algorithm | Best | Average | Worst | Space | Stable | Notes |
|-----------|------|---------|-------|-------|--------|-------|
| Bubble sort | O(n) | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(1) | yes | Pedagogical only. |
| Insertion sort | O(n) | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(1) | yes | Excellent on small / nearly-sorted inputs. |
| Selection sort | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(1) | no | Worst-case-friendly but no early exit. |
| Mergesort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | **O(n)** | **yes** | Predictable; external-sort friendly. |
| Heapsort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(1) | no | In-place; not as cache-friendly. |
| Quicksort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | **O(n²)** | O(log n) | no | Very fast in practice; pivot choice matters. |
| **Timsort** (Python) | O(n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n) | **yes** | Adaptive; the default in `sorted`. |
| Counting sort | — | O(n + k) | O(n + k) | O(k) | yes | Integers in a known range. |
| Radix sort | — | O(n · d) | O(n · d) | O(n + k) | yes | Multi-digit keys; d = digits. |

### Stability

A **stable** sort preserves the relative order of equal-keyed elements. That matters when you sort by multiple keys:

```python
# Sort by score desc, then by id asc — using stability:
rows.sort(key=lambda r: r.id)              # secondary
rows.sort(key=lambda r: -r.score)          # primary  (stable wrt the previous)
```

### In-place vs out-of-place

- `list.sort()` mutates the list in place; returns `None`.
- `sorted(iterable)` returns a new list; works on any iterable.

### Why this matters for Gen AI

- **Ranking retrieved chunks** by similarity score before assembling context.
- **Top-k via heap** vs full sort — when k ≪ n, `heapq.nlargest(k, ...)` is O(n log k), cheaper than O(n log n).
- **Token-level batching** — sort by sequence length to pad efficiently (length-bucketed batching).
- **Eval reproducibility** — stable sorts give deterministic outputs across runs, which makes regression tests reliable.
- **External / out-of-core sorting** — when logs or embeddings don't fit in RAM, mergesort-based external sorting is the workhorse.
keyPoints: Comparison sorts are bounded below by Ω(n log n); non-comparison sorts can be O(n) for structured keys.
Python's sorted/list.sort use Timsort — stable, adaptive, O(n log n) worst case, O(n) on nearly-sorted input.
Stability matters for multi-key sorts: sort by secondary key first, then by primary.
For top-k with k ≪ n, prefer heapq.nlargest (O(n log k)) over full sorting.
Quicksort is O(n²) worst case; pick mergesort/Timsort when worst-case latency matters.
